questions.question: Define mean for grouped data using the direct method.
questions.answer: Mean = (Σf×x)/Σf, where f is the frequency of each class and x is the class midpoint.

questions.question: Define median for grouped data.
questions.answer: Median = L + [(N/2 - CF)/f] × h, where L = lower boundary of median class, N = total frequency, CF = cumulative frequency before median class, f = frequency of median class, h = class width.

questions.question: Define mode for grouped data.
questions.answer: Mode = L + [(f1 - f0)/(2f1 - f0 - f2)] × h, where L = lower boundary of modal class, f1 = frequency of modal class, f0 = frequency of previous class, f2 = frequency of next class, h = class width.

questions.question: What is the difference between mean, median and mode?
questions.answer: Mean is the average of data, median is the middle value, mode is the most frequent value.

questions.question: How do you find cumulative frequency for a data set?
questions.answer: Cumulative frequency is found by adding the frequency of each class to the sum of the frequencies of all preceding classes.

questions.question: Explain less than and more than ogives.
questions.answer: Less than ogive plots cumulative frequencies up to the upper class boundary; More than ogive plots cumulative frequencies from the upper class boundary downward.

questions.question: How can you estimate the mean from a histogram?
questions.answer: Take the midpoints of each class, multiply by corresponding frequencies, sum them and divide by total frequency.

questions.question: How do you find the mode from a histogram?
questions.answer: Mode is estimated as the value corresponding to the highest bar in the histogram (modal class).

questions.question: What is a cumulative frequency table?
questions.answer: A cumulative frequency table shows the running total of frequencies for all classes up to that point.

questions.question: What is the relationship between median and cumulative frequency?
questions.answer: Median corresponds to the class where cumulative frequency reaches N/2, where N is total frequency.

questions.question: Explain the empirical relation between mean, median, and mode.
questions.answer: Empirical relation: Mode ≈ 3 Median - 2 Mean for moderately skewed data.

questions.question: What is the cumulative frequency of the last class?
questions.answer: The cumulative frequency of the last class equals the total frequency (N) of the data set.

questions.question: How is frequency polygon drawn from a histogram?
questions.answer: Mark midpoints of each class on X-axis, plot corresponding frequencies on Y-axis, join points with straight lines.

questions.question: How do you estimate median from an ogive?
questions.answer: Locate N/2 on Y-axis, draw a horizontal line to meet the ogive, drop a vertical to X-axis to read the median value.

questions.question: What is class width?
questions.answer: Class width (h) = upper class boundary - lower class boundary.

questions.question: What is the modal class?
questions.answer: The class with the highest frequency is called the modal class.

questions.question: How can you tell if data is skewed using mean, median, and mode?
questions.answer: If Mean > Median > Mode, data is positively skewed; If Mean < Median < Mode, data is negatively skewed.

questions.question: What is the class mark used for in calculations?
questions.answer: Class mark is used as a representative value of a class for estimating mean or other statistics.

questions.question: What is the difference between less than ogive and more than ogive?
questions.answer: Less than ogive cumulates frequencies from lowest up, more than ogive cumulates from highest down.

questions.question: Explain how to calculate mean using assumed mean method.
questions.answer: Mean = A + Σf*d/Σf, where A = assumed mean, d = deviation of midpoints from A, f = frequency.

questions.question: How do you find cumulative frequency less than a class?
questions.answer: Sum all frequencies of classes whose upper boundary is less than the given class.

questions.question: How do you find cumulative frequency more than a class?
questions.answer: Subtract cumulative frequency up to that class from total frequency.

questions.question: How is a histogram different from a bar graph?
questions.answer: Histogram uses contiguous bars for continuous data; bar graph uses separated bars for discrete data.

questions.question: What is the effect of changing class width on frequency distribution?
questions.answer: Increasing class width decreases number of classes, may reduce detail; decreasing width increases number of classes, more detail.

questions.question: Why do we use cumulative frequency?
questions.answer: Cumulative frequency helps in estimating median, percentiles, and understanding distribution of data.
